title: Dog Circular Hair Loss Guide: Causes and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Find out what causes circular hair loss in dogs and when to see a vet. Learn why this low-urgency condition often benefits from photo triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Circular hair loss in dogs is typically caused by manageable conditions like ringworm or mange and usually requires a routine vet visit rather than emergency care. Sharing a photo with your clinic helps the veterinary team assess the urgency and prepare the necessary diagnostic tests.

What could be causing the circular patches of hair loss on my dog?

  • Ringworm, which is a contagious fungal infection
  • Microscopic skin mites, commonly known as mange
  • Localized bacterial infections, such as folliculitis
  • A localized reaction to an insect bite

Is my dog’s circular hair loss considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level is generally low and not considered a life-threatening emergency.
  • Seek immediate care only if the hair loss is accompanied by lethargy, loss of appetite, or extreme skin swelling.
  • Schedule a routine appointment within a few days to prevent the condition from spreading to other pets or humans.

How does providing a photo of the hair loss help with my dog's triage?

  • Clear images allow veterinary professionals to examine the skin's texture and look for inflammation or crusting.
  • Photos help identify specific patterns of hair loss to determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.
  • Visual information helps the clinic prepare for diagnostic tests, such as skin scrapings or fungal cultures, before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Focal areas of alopecia, typically 0.5-2 cm in diameter, may develop due to affected hairs epilating. Mild epidermal collarette formation may be present at the margins of hair loss, but follicular pustules and erythema are often absent, particularly in short-haired breeds, complicating diagnosis. Certain breeds, such as Collies and Shetland Sheepdogs, can present with widespread alopecia, mild erythema, and epidermal collarette formation, mimicking endocrinopathies; pustules and crusts are infrequently found. Diagnostic steps include skin scrapings for fleas, mites, and bacteria; impression smears for bacterial or yeast infections; fungal cultures for dermatophytosis; and examination of plucked hairs for evidence of dermatophytosis or chewed hair shafts. If initial tests are inconclusive, skin biopsies are indicated to evaluate hair follicle structures, numbers, and anagen/telogen ratios, as well as to identify bacterial, fungal, parasitic, inflammatory, or neoplastic causes. Bacterial pyoderma is a common cause of abnormal shedding and alopecia.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 864)

faqs.question: Is circular hair loss in dogs contagious?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. Ringworm is highly contagious to other animals and humans, whereas mange or allergic reactions are typically not.

faqs.question: Can I treat my dog's hair loss at home?
faqs.answer: It is best to wait for a diagnosis from a veterinarian before applying any ointments, as using the wrong product can sometimes irritate the skin further.

faqs.question: Will the hair eventually grow back?
faqs.answer: Yes, in the vast majority of cases, once the underlying infection or parasite is successfully treated, the hair will begin to grow back within a few weeks.
